  • Thu 23 Apr 2026 by João Neto

    Call for Papers

    The Calls for Papers for the Research and Engineering tracks of SCAM 2026 are now available. Please visit the respective track pages for full details.

All News Articles

SCAM 2026

The aim of the IEEE International Conference on Source Code Analysis & Manipulation (SCAM) is to bring together researchers and practitioners working on theory, techniques and applications that concern analysis and/or manipulation of the source code of computer systems. While much attention in the wider software engineering community is properly directed towards other aspects of systems development and evolution, such as specification, design, and requirements engineering, it is the source code that contains the only precise description of the behavior of the system. The analysis and manipulation of source code thus remains a pressing concern.

For the purpose of clarity, ‘source code’ is taken to mean any fully executable description of a software system. It is therefore so-construed as to include machine code, very high level languages and executable graphical representations of systems. The term ‘analysis’ is taken to mean any automated or semi automated procedure which takes source code and yields insight into its meaning. The term ‘manipulation’ is taken to mean any automated or semi-automated procedure which takes and returns source code.

— Steering Committee Charter

The 26th IEEE International Conference on Source Code Analysis and Manipulation (SCAM 2026) will be held on September 14-15 in Benevento, Italy, co-located with ICSME 2026.

SCAM 2026 will feature two tracks:

  • Research Track
  • Engineering Track

Engineering Track: Call for Papers

View track page for all details

We invite submissions describing industrial applications, case studies, tools, and experience reports related to source code analysis and manipulation.

Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:

  • Static and dynamic analysis in practice
  • Program transformation and refactoring in industrial systems
  • Program comprehension and reverse engineering
  • Mining software repositories in industry
  • AI/ML for source code analysis and manipulation (with clear relevance to SCAM topics)
  • Tool support for software maintenance and evolution
  • Continuous integration and DevOps involving code analysis
  • Software quality, testing, and debugging in practice
  • Large-scale system maintenance and modernization
  • Lessons learned, best practices, and negative results

Submissions must clearly demonstrate practical relevance and impact.

Submission Types

Full Papers (10 pages + up to 2 pages for references)

Full papers should present mature industrial contributions and must:

  • Address a problem of practical importance
  • Clearly describe the industrial or real-world context
  • Explain the approach, techniques, and methodologies used
  • Provide evidence supporting the conclusions (empirical or experience-based)
  • Discuss impact, effectiveness, and lessons learned

Submissions should be technically sound and practically relevant. Authors are encouraged to highlight innovation, efficiency, and applicability.

Short Papers (3–5 pages + 1 page for references)

Short papers may include:

  • Emerging or unsolved industrial challenges
  • Experience reports (successes or failures)
  • Applications of existing research techniques in practice
  • Early-stage ideas with strong practical relevance

We especially encourage submissions describing lessons learned from applying academic approaches in industrial contexts.

Submission Guidelines

  • Submissions must describe original, unpublished work and must not be under review elsewhere.
  • Papers must be written in English and submitted in PDF format via EasyChair (link to be announced).
  • Papers must conform to the IEEE conference proceedings format:
  • LaTeX: \documentclass\[10pt,conference\]{IEEEtran}
  • No compsoc or compsocconf options
  • Templates are available on the IEEE website.

If a paper is accepted, at least one author must register and present the paper at the conference.

All authors, reviewers, and organizers must comply with the IEEE Code of Conduct and IEEE Submission and Peer Review Policies, including guidelines on AI-generated text.

Review Process (Single-Blind)

The Engineering Track follows a single-blind review process:

  • Reviewer identities are hidden from authors
  • Author names and affiliations are visible to reviewers

Each submission will be reviewed by members of the program committee and evaluated based on:

  • Relevance to SCAM topics
  • Practical significance and industrial impact
  • Technical quality and soundness
  • Clarity and completeness

Policy on AI/ML Papers

Papers involving AI or ML techniques must explicitly demonstrate how they address a source code analysis and/or manipulation problem.

Submissions that do not clearly establish this connection may be desk rejected or considered out of scope.

Artifacts and Reproducibility

We strongly encourage submissions accompanied by:

  • Tools
  • Datasets
  • Replication packages

Artifacts that support reproducibility and practical adoption are highly valued.

Proceedings

All accepted papers will appear in the SCAM 2026 proceedings, published by IEEE and made available through IEEE Xplore.

Presentation

At least one author of each accepted paper is expected to:

  • Register for the conference
  • Attend and present the paper

In exceptional circumstances, remote presentations may be considered.

Research Track: Call for Papers

View track page for all details

Covered Topics and Paper Formats

We welcome submission of papers that describe original and significant work in the field of source code analysis and manipulation. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:

  • abstract interpretation
  • bad smell detection
  • bug location and prediction
  • clone detection
  • concern, concept, and feature localization and mining
  • decompilation
  • energy efficient source code
  • natural language analysis of source code artifacts
  • program comprehension
  • program slicing
  • program transformation and refactoring
  • repository, revision, and change analysis
  • security vulnerability analysis
  • source level metrics
  • source level optimization
  • source-level testing and verification
  • static and dynamic analysis

SCAM explicitly solicits results from any theoretical or technological domain that can be applied to these and similar topics. Submitted papers should describe original, unpublished, and significant work and must not have been previously accepted for publication nor be concurrently submitted for review in another journal, book, conference, or workshop.

Submission will be reviewed by at least three members of the program committee, judging the paper on its novelty, quality, importance, evaluation, and scientific rigor. If the paper is accepted, at least one author must register for the conference and present the paper. All authors, reviewers, and organizers are expected to uphold the IEEE Code of Conduct. Failure to do so may lead to a (desk) rejection of the paper.

Papers involving AI or ML must explicitly explain how they address a source code analysis and manipulation problem. Papers not meeting this criterion may be more suitable for AI- or ML-focused venues instead, and will be (desk) rejected.

Submission guidelines

All submissions must be in English and should be submitted electronically in PDF format using EasyChair.

  • Papers must not exceed 12 pages, and the last 2 pages can be used for references only.
  • Papers must conform to the IEEE proceedings paper format guidelines (LaTeX users must use \documentclass[10pt,conference]{IEEEtran} without including the compsoc or compsocconf options)
  • Templates in Latex and Word are available on IEEE’s website.
  • All submissions must be in English and should be submitted electronically in PDF format.
  • If the paper is accepted, at least one author must register for the conference and present the paper.
  • All authors, reviewers, and organizers are expected to uphold the IEEE Code of Conduct and the IEEE Submission and Peer Review Policies, including their Guidelines on AI-Generated Text. Failure to do so by an author may lead to a (desk) rejection of the paper.
Double-blind Review

We follow a double-blind reviewing process. Submitted papers must adhere to the following rules:

  • Author names and affiliations must be omitted. (The track co-chairs will check compliance before reviewing begins.)
  • References to authors’ own related work must be in the third person. (For example, not “We build on our previous work…” but rather “We build on the work of…”)

If the program chairs find that the authors did not respect the rules of double-blind review they can decide to (desk) reject the paper.

Artifacts

We welcome papers accompanied by comprehensive and well-documented artifacts to enable reproduction.

Proceedings

All accepted papers will appear in the proceedings which will be available through IEEE XPlore.

Presentation

We expect at least one author to register for the conference and attend to present their paper. In exceptional circumstances beyond the authors’ control, we may consider remote presentations.
